Front: RPA 6 force and extension: method for investigating a spring
Back:
- Set up a clamp stand with a spring hanging from the clamp
- Fix a metre rule beside the spring with a second clamp and boss
- Record the ruler reading level with the bottom of the unstretched spring
- Add a known weight to the spring
- Record the new position of the bottom of the spring
- Calculate extension = new length/reading - original length/reading
- Add more weights so the force increases step by step
- Record the spring position and extension for each force
- Plot force against extension or use F = k e
- Risk: masses/clamp could fall or spring could break; secure apparatus and wear goggles

Front: RPA 6 force and extension: context, risks and examiner traps
Back:
- Extension is change in length, not total length
- Clamp the ruler vertically and keep it fixed
- Read the bottom of the spring at eye level to reduce parallax error
- Several forces help identify anomalous results or reduce random error
- Repeating alone was not enough in one mark scheme unless linked to anomalies/random error
- Hooke's law is supported by a straight line through the origin
- Use F = k e
- Convert cm to m when calculating spring constant in N/m
- Do not go beyond the limit of proportionality if testing Hooke's law

Front: RPA 7 force, mass and acceleration: trolley method
Back:
- Use a trolley on a runway connected by string over a pulley to a mass hanger
- Clamp the pulley to the edge of the desk so the string does not rub
- Add a slotted mass to the mass holder
- Use W = m g to calculate the pulling force
- Mark start and finish points on the runway
- Measure the distance between the marks with a metre rule
- Release the trolley without pushing it
- Measure the time with a stopclock/timer, light gates/data logger or ticker timer
- Calculate acceleration from speed/time data or let the data logger calculate it
- Repeat for different slotted masses or different ramp heights
- Repeat readings and calculate a mean

Front: RPA 7 force, mass and acceleration: context, risks and examiner traps
Back:
- Trilogy Physics has 6-mark RPA 7 method questions for trolley acceleration
- 2023 Trilogy tests varying force with a pulley, string and mass holder
- 2020 Trilogy tests how ramp height affects acceleration
- For constant mass, acceleration is directly proportional to resultant force
- For constant resultant force, acceleration is inversely proportional to mass
- Use resultant force = mass x acceleration
- Use W = m g to calculate pulling force from hanging mass
- Release from the same position each time and do not push the trolley
- Keep the relevant mass controlled when varying force
- Use repeats, discard anomalies and calculate a mean
- Uncertainty from repeats can be ± half the range

Front: RPA 8 waves: method for measuring wave speed
Back:
- Trilogy Physics has a 6-mark water-wave method for measuring wavelength, frequency and speed
- For frequency, count waves passing a point in a fixed time
- Divide time by number of waves to find period T, then use f = 1/T
- Alternatively, read frequency from the oscillator
- For wavelength, use a camera to freeze the image
- Measure distance across several wavefronts with a metre rule
- Divide the distance by the number of waves to find wavelength
- For speed, use v = f x wavelength
- Alternatively, time one wavefront over a measured screen distance and use speed = distance ÷ time
- For waves on a string, adjust frequency, keep masses the same, move the bridge and get a stationary pattern

Front: RPA 8 waves: context, risks and examiner traps
Back:
- Trilogy Physics has a 6-mark RPA 8 water-wave question and 4-mark string-wave method questions
- To access top level in the water-wave question, describe frequency, wavelength and speed
- Measure wavelength across several waves, not just one gap, then divide
- Use a metre rule or tape measure for large wavelengths
- Convert cm or mm to m when needed for v = fλ
- Frequency is waves per second, not wavelength
- If wave speed is constant and frequency increases, wavelength decreases
- A stationary wave pattern is needed before measuring wavelength on a string
- Precise results have a small spread about the mean
- Do not just name apparatus; say how each measurement is taken

Front: RPA 9 reflection/refraction: refraction method with a glass block
Back:
- Place a glass block on a piece of paper
- Draw around the glass block
- Shine a narrow ray from a ray box through the glass block
- Mark the incident ray entering the block
- Mark the emergent ray leaving the block
- Remove the block and join the points to show the full ray path
- Draw a normal at 90° to the surface where the ray enters
- Use a protractor to measure angle of incidence and angle of refraction
- Repeat for different angles of incidence, for example 10° intervals
- Plot or compare the results

Front: RPA 9 reflection/refraction: context, risks and examiner traps
Back:
- Measure angles from the normal, not from the surface
- A narrow ray is more accurate because the centre of the ray is easier to judge
- Mark two points on each ray so the path can be joined accurately
- A protractor with 1° resolution should not be used to record values to 1 decimal place
- Refraction data may need a curved line of best fit, not a straight line
- A ray along the normal does not refract because it enters the glass at the same time/along the normal
- For reflection, angle of incidence equals angle of reflection
- In one refraction method question, mirror/reflection methods scored zero because the question asked for refraction

Front: RPA 10 infrared radiation and surfaces: method
Back:
- Use two flasks or surfaces with different finishes, such as black and silver
- For emission, put equal volumes of hot water in each flask
- Put thermometers in the flasks or place IR detectors near them
- Record initial temperature or detector reading
- Start a stopclock
- Record temperatures or detector readings after the same time, such as 10 minutes
- Calculate temperature decrease or change in detector reading for each flask
- Compare the changes to test the hypothesis
- Keep distance from detector/heater the same
- Keep starting conditions the same

Front: RPA 10 infrared radiation and surfaces: context, risks and examiner traps
Back:
- Black surfaces absorb more infrared than white surfaces
- Matt surfaces absorb more infrared than shiny surfaces of the same colour
- Matt black is often the best absorber; shiny white is often the worst absorber
- For emission, black surfaces usually emit infrared better than silver/shiny surfaces
- Compare temperature change, not just final temperature
- Greatest emission rate is when the temperature difference from surroundings is greatest
- Repeat readings are needed to show precision by clustering closely
- Low detector resolution can weaken a conclusion if two readings round to the same value
- Heater/infrared radiation is the hazard; burns are the risk
